author: @jerryfacts9749
text: If you have actual Piezzo quartz stone cut as a flat piece, and if you were to properly set it up with contacts on opposite sides it can produce a few thousandths of a Volt for an instant. The pulse width would be dependent on the resonance of the stone, force of the strike, and anything being used to hold it in to position. This would happen when you strike the stone.  The current output would be in micro Amps or a few millionths of an Amp.  You would need an oscilloscope to see this.  Set the time base to about 10 to 15 HZ per cm range. Input sensitivity should be set to about 10 mv/cm.  When striking the quart stone you would see the trace jump.  With a digital storage scope you can capture the waveform and take a measurement.

text: Hilarious! Reminds me of a prank  report I sent off to the safety committee years ago. I went on to criticize the safety committee for not including the MSDS report on this substance in the appropriate areas around the plant. I intended this to be a joke and so as to prevent it from going too far I included a member of the committee in on the joke. Well things went too far... The "pot boiled over"  my safety valve failed me and the situation got out of control. text: Rob, have you seen the Garuda Sanjivani videos? It is apparently a Root or stick that can travel against the flow of water. I tried to replicate it and 3d print but i had no luck as my attempt was heavy and sunk.  Would love to see a video on your thoughts. replies.author: @dirtboater1
replies.text: "Crystal" radios didn't use quartz, they used Galena.   The "crystal" doesn't produce power, it just rectifies the incoming signal.  Simple crystal radios can work without any batteries or amplifiers if you use a high impedance earpiece.

replies.author: @wbeaty
replies.text:
@ThinkingandTinkering Another trick:  HITTING TWO WHITE QUARTZ STONES TOGETHER IS NOT ELECTRICAL!  Do it in a dark room, and notice that the dim flash is yellow, rather than the blue/violet of plasma.   Also notice that, after hitting the stones, the contact-point smells burned.   What if it's all frictional?  Maybe it's like the "sparks" from grinding wheels, which aren't electrical of course.

If the tiny contact-point between quartz pebbles is getting hot enough via friction, it will glow yellow-white for a moment.   But in that case, ANY stone should do the same  ...but the vast majority are not translucent!  Any flash will be concealed, hidden down between the two opaque walls coming together.   In order to flash, at least one of the stones must be translucent.   (And also, a violent **scrape** might give out much more light, while a perfect **strike** with no sliding motion, might not produce any flash at all.   That's if the flash is entirely caused by frictional heating.)

So, try it with a white translucent mineral which isn't piezo.   Or, perhaps try it with milky glass.

Heh, maybe try spinning a tiny object in a dremel, then pushing it hard against some translucent  ...marble, or glass, etc.   Will a tiny drill-bit or a grinding-wheel produce some light, when it's against a translucent white object?

author: @TonyGingrich
text:
Most of these shams are easy enough for even myself to see coming around the corner. I think they're amusing, nonetheless. Like watching Capt. Kirk and crew fly a Klingon ship around the Sun to travel back in time...courtesy of Spock's "calculations".

But I'll tell you what really annoys me... I am currently doing mental homework in preparation to build a from-scratch electrical generator. Project start date, as yet to be determined. Why? Because gathering useful information is so painfully slow. Try typing "generator from scratch" in YouTube's search bar. Let me know when you find something not related to "free energy". Take your time. I won't hold my breath.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: I have done loads of from scratch stuff and seen loads - where are you looking?

replies.author: @TonyGingrich
replies.text:
@@RobertMurraySmith  I don't know how to provide a brief answer. Could possibly email with more detail. But this is an over-simplification.

To summarize: An electrical generator as a "sole provider" for an average home, with respectable overload capacity. Somewhere in the 30 - 50 kilowatt range.

With that goal, I've been looking more for industrial designs. Youtube, Google, wikipedia...the usual lineup. The most respectable information I can find, tends to be focused on portable generator repairs and rewinds. Which, those have been informative. But those are focused more on designs for less than 100% duty cycles. 2 key differences between portables and industrials seem to be in the rotor poles and stator windings.

I'm more attracted to 4-pole designs, because I envision an alternator which can be rotated by an open-ended range of power sources. 1,800 vs 3,600 rpm seems to be more strategic.

The winding options are a bit more nebulous to me. Solid bars instead of coiled wires, seems to offer more towards maintenance and minimal downtime. Also better heat tolerances.

Excitation was previously a mystery to me, but I think I'm now comfortable with my understanding and ideas on that. But I would like some good information on specific areas, such as speed/voltage relationships, as well as how laminated iron cores affect the magnetic flux lines.

Like I said...it's a lot, ha!
